text:
Membership in research studies remains a disproportionately minority population experience, a trend that becomes increasingly important as cancer and chronic diseases continue to plague minority communities. Recruitment efforts in the US are hindered by many barriers including, mistrust of the medical and research communities, linguistic and cultural barriers, and socioeconomic disadvantage. Our studies apply a community-based recruitment strategy, grounded in principles of global health research. We train and hire members from the populations of interest to be research assistants (RAs) and use them as the primary interface with study subjects. Because RAs share language, culture and social determinants with potential participants, they function as a critical translational bridge between investigators and community members.

We have been applying this approach since 2001 in the Texas Rio Grande Valley. Through sustained engagement with the community, we established a longitudinal cohort of more than 5,000 Mexican Americans that were derived from a random sample of 12,000 households. Our initial team of research assistants (RAs) received basic training. Currently, this team comprises over 30 highly trained and experienced personnel capable of performing sophisticated clinical assessments, including ultrasound, FibroScan, retinal photography, and cognition assessments. We are currently funded by multiple NIH awards to investigate liver disease, obesity and diabetes in our cohort. By integrating multi-omics research with the trust in the community we are establishing a model of precision medicine that will lead to successful disease prevention and treatment through highly individualized interventions that are also community-inclusive for underserved populations.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
